algorithms.title: Pediatric Tachycardia With a Pulse — Decision Algorithm
algorithms.description: Stepwise approach to evaluating and managing a pediatric patient with a heart rate above the normal range for age, based on the 2025 AHA PALS tachycardia algorithm.
steps: Step 1 — Confirm tachycardia: heart rate above the upper limit of normal for the patient's age.
Step 2 — Assess perfusion and hemodynamic stability: check mental status, skin color and temperature, pulse quality, capillary refill, and blood pressure.
Step 3 — If UNSTABLE (poor perfusion, hypotension, altered mental status, shock, heart failure): proceed immediately to synchronized cardioversion at 0.5–1 J/kg.
Step 4 — If the first cardioversion attempt fails: increase energy to 2 J/kg and repeat synchronized cardioversion.
Step 5 — If STABLE: obtain a 12-lead ECG if possible to assess QRS width and rhythm morphology.
Step 6 — Narrow-complex tachycardia (QRS < 0.09 s): suspect SVT if sudden onset, regular, rate 220–300 bpm (infants) or 150–250 bpm (children), no visible P waves.
Step 7 — Attempt adenosine: 0.1 mg/kg rapid IV push through a proximal IV, followed by saline flush (maximum first dose 6 mg).
Step 8 — If adenosine fails: second dose at 0.2 mg/kg rapid IV push (maximum 12 mg).
Step 9 — If adenosine fails twice: cardiology consultation; consider verapamil (in children > 1 year), amiodarone, or synchronized cardioversion.
Step 10 — Wide-complex tachycardia (QRS ≥ 0.09 s): consider SVT with aberrancy versus ventricular tachycardia.
Step 11 — If stable and uncertain diagnosis: cardiology consultation before administering antiarrhythmics.
Step 12 — If stable and VT suspected with structural heart disease: amiodarone 5 mg/kg IV over 20–60 minutes with cardiac monitoring.
Step 13 — After successful conversion: continuous monitoring, cardiology follow-up, and evaluation for underlying substrate.
